coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for simultaneous detection of L-ascorbic acid and alkaline phosphatase activity based on red carbon dots/polydopamine nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Qi
Mu, Pingping
Ning, Gan
Zhang, Wenqian
Li, Bo
Huang, Shan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, efficient red carbon dots (R-CDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment of N-(4-amino phenyl) acetamide and (2,3-difluoro phenyl) boronic acid. The optimal emission peak of R-CDs was at 602 nm (under 520 nm excitation) and the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of R-CDs was 12.9%. Polydopamine, which was formed by the self-polymerization and cyclization of dopamine in alkaline condition, emitted characteristic fluorescence with peak position of 517 nm (under 420 nm excitation) and affected the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs through inner filter effect. L-Ascorbic acid (AA), which was the hydrolysis product of L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate trisodium salt under the catalytic reaction of alkaline phosphatase (ALP), effectively prevented the polymerization of dopamine. Combined with the ALP-mediated AA production and the AA-mediated polydopamine generation, the ratiometric fluorescence signal of polydopamine with R-CDs was correlated closely with the concentration of both AA and ALP. Under optimal conditions, the detection limits of AA and ALP were 0.28 μM during linear range of 0.5–30 μM and 0.044 U/L with linear range of 0.05–8 U/L, respectively. This ratiometric fluorescence detection platform can efficiently shield the background interference of sophisticated samples by introducing a self-calibration as reference signal in a multi-excitation mode, which can detect AA and ALP in human serum samples with satisfactory results. Such R-CDs/polydopamine nanocomposite provides a steadfast quantitative information and makes R-CDs be excellent candidate for biosensors via combining target recognition strategy.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for simultaneous detection of L-ascorbic acid and alkaline phosphatase activity based on red carbon dots/polydopamine nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning, 530001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning, 530001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, efficient red carbon dots (R-CDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment of N-(4-amino phenyl) acetamide and (2,3-difluoro phenyl) boronic acid. The optimal emission peak of R-CDs was at 602 nm (under 520 nm excitation) and the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of R-CDs was 12.9%. Polydopamine, which was formed by the self-polymerization and cyclization of dopamine in alkaline condition, emitted characteristic fluorescence with peak position of 517 nm (under 420 nm excitation) and affected the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs through inner filter effect. L-Ascorbic acid (AA), which was the hydrolysis product of L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate trisodium salt under the catalytic reaction of alkaline phosphatase (ALP), effectively prevented the polymerization of dopamine. Combined with the ALP-mediated AA production and the AA-mediated polydopamine generation, the ratiometric fluorescence signal of polydopamine with R-CDs was correlated closely with the concentration of both AA and ALP. Under optimal conditions, the detection limits of AA and ALP were 0.28 μM during linear range of 0.5–30 μM and 0.044 U/L with linear range of 0.05–8 U/L, respectively. This ratiometric fluorescence detection platform can efficiently shield the background interference of sophisticated samples by introducing a self-calibration as reference signal in a multi-excitation mode, which can detect AA and ALP in human serum samples with satisfactory results. Such R-CDs/polydopamine nanocomposite provides a steadfast quantitative information and makes R-CDs be excellent candidate for biosensors via combining target recognition strategy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a kind of zero-dimensional carbon material, have been accidently discovered by Xu et al., in 2004 [1] and have attracted significant attention from researchers due to their outstanding photostability, superior biocompatibility, and excellent optical properties [2,3]. In general, the application areas of CDs are mainly in the fields of photocatalysis, bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery and release, cancer diagnosis, and disease treatment [4,5]. The synthesis approaches of CDs can be divided into two kinds of top-down methods and down-top methods. Top-down methods usually include arc discharge method [1] and laser ablation method [6]. Down-top methods mainly contain the hydrothermal method [7], solvothermal method [8], and microwave pyrolysis method [9]. However, most of these CDs possess short emission wavelengths mainly in the blue or green emission ranges (<600 nm) [10]. The UV light excitation of CDs may cause severe light damage to cells and tissues, and the fluorescent signal of CDs can be also affected by the autofluorescence of the biological sample matrix, which seriously hinders the wider application of these CDs in bioanalysis and biosensing fields [5]. Therefore, it is still a big challenge and a great demand to synthesize high-efficient CDs with longer emission wavelength, such as red CDs (R-CDs). Shi et al. used neutral red and thiourea as precursors to prepare R-CDs successfully via hydrothermal method without complicated operations and abundant solvents [11]. However, some reported hydrothermal methods still need complicated operations and/or abundant solvents. For instance, Li et al. used dimethylformamide as the solvent to treat citric acid and thiourea to synthesize blue CDs, and then they adjusted these blue CDs by sodium hydroxide to synthesize R-CDs [8]. Finally, the modified R-CDs were obtained by adjusting the CDs with hydrochloric acid. Shan et al. synthesized R-CDs through thermal-heating method by using N,N-diethyl formamide as solvent and both citric acid and urea as precursors, and the final mixture was purified by silica gel column chromatography [12]. These procedures take long time and require great patience, and it is extremely complicated and may generate plenty of waste. Therefore, it is urgent to develop an efficient and relatively simple synthetic route for the preparation of R-CDs.

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), which is a hydrolase with high selectivity for phosphate-containing substrates in alkaline substances, is essential enzyme for bacteria and organisms and plays a vital role in normal physiological functions [13]. Usually, the normal amount of ALP in human serum sample is reported in the range of 46–190 U/L [14]. ALP with abnormal levels will cause many diseases, such as bone disease [15], prostate cancer [16], and diabetes [17]. It is well-known that L-ascorbic acid (AA) takes vital role in several biochemical processes and often serves as an effective therapeutic drug to treat the free radical-induced diseases [18]. AA is also an enzyme cofactor and is involved in neurotransmitter-related enzymes for nutrition [19]. The reported concentration of AA in human serum sample is in the range of 43.8–487 μM [19–21]. AA with the excess amount may cause diarrhea, taste/aroma deterioration, gastric irritation, and kidney calculi [22]. A variety of methods have been developed for the AA and ALP detection, such as colorimetry [23,24], high performance liquid chromatography [25], electrochemistry [26,27], fluorescence immunoassay methods [28], and fluorescence methods [22,29,30]. Among these detection methods, fluorescence methods have attracted great attention due to their superior advantages such as flexibility, convenience, efficiency, and the elimination of complicated training operations. For example, Li et al. prepared blue CDs via one-step hydrothermal method and used such fluorescent CDs to detect ALP as low as 0.02 U/L [29]. In their work, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by p-nitrophenol (PNP) via inner filter effect (IFE), and PNP was produced by the hydrolyzation of p-nitrophenyl phosphate (PNPP) under the catalysis of ALP. Chen et al. prepared pH-responsive CDs for the detections of nitrite and AA via the fluorescence turn-off and turn-on assay [30]. However, this mono-signal fluorescent detection strategy possesses short emission wavelength, poor accuracy, and relatively low sensitivity, which makes them are not suitable for the ultrasensitive detection of low-abundance target in sophisticated matrixes. For the reason that, it is more important to design and develop a better detection assay for AA and ALP based on double-signal fluorescent detection platform. Huang and Shui et al. established a ratiometric fluorescent assays for the detection of ALP activity with excellent results [31,32]. Compared with mono-signal fluorescent detection method, ratiometric fluorescent detection strategy is more accurate, practical, reliable, and effective, and it also shows better anti-interference ability to expand the scope of application.

Enlighten by the pioneering literatures, we designed and synthesized R-CDs with excellent stability and superior optical properties by one-step hydrothermal treatment of N-(4-amino phenyl) acetamide and (2,3-difluoro phenyl) boronic acid without solvent treatment and complex processing steps. The ratiometric fluorescent probe was constitutive of R-CDs and polydopamine (PDA) which was self-polymerized and cyclized by dopamine (DA) in alkaline condition. AA, which is the hydrolysis product of L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate trisodium salt (AAP) under the catalysis of ALP, can prevent the polymerization of DA and then inhibit the generation of PDA, leading to the variation of the ratiometric fluorescence signal. Due to the strong and specific enzyme-substrate interactions, such assay can simultaneously detect both AA and ALP activity with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. To our knowledge, this work is the first report to detect both AA and ALP activity via the R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite-based ratiometric fluorescent probe, and the proposed detection platform can be expanded to the application of other important targets detection and provide experience for new sensing strategies.

AA, ALP, AAP, DA, N-(4-amino phenyl) acetamide, (2,3-difluoro phenyl) boronic acid, PNP, PNPP, N-acetyl maleimide (NEM), acetylcholine esterase (AChE), glucose oxidase (GOx), cholesterol oxidase (ChOx), reduced glutathione (GSH), oxidized glutathione (GSSG), l-cysteine (L-Cys), other enzymes, proteins, amino acids, and various inorganic salts were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. The reagents were all analytically pure and were used without further purification. The water used in the laboratory is purified by the ultrapure water system of Millipore (Millipore, 18.2MΩ cm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of R-CDs and PDA
ce:section.ce:para: In a word, 0.15 g of N-(4-amino phenyl) acetamide and 0.07 g of (2,3-difluoro phenyl) boronic acid were mixed with 20 mL of ultrapure water for ultrasonication for 5 min. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to the polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and was reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. The crude product was centrifuged at 9867 g for 20 min, and the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter. The filtrate was dialyzed with a 500 Da dialysis bag for 2 days. The solid R-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying the liquid in the dialysis bag.

The optical properties of R-CDs and PDA were characterized on UV3600 plus spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), RF6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), QM-8075 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba, Japan) with time-correlated single-photo counting system, and FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The structures of R-CDs were characterized on JEM-2011 EFF field transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan), D&Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany), Escalab 250 Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Zeta potentials of R-CDs and PDA were measured on Nano particle size analyzer SZ-100 (Horiba, Japan). The structure of PDA was characterized on JEM-2011 EFF field transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan). Mass spectrum of PDA were recorded with Bruker ultrafiltrate MALDI-TOF mass spectrometer (Bruker, Germany).

For AA detection, 150 μL of freshly prepared DA (60 mM), 20 μL of R-CDs (0.10 mg/mL), and 50 μL of AA with different concentration were mixed thoroughly. Then, the mixture was diluted to be 3 mL with Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH 10.0) and further incubated at room temperature for 25 min. The fluorescence spectra with excitation wavelengths of 420 nm and 520 nm were recorded for AA analysis.

For ALP activity detection, 100 μL of ALP with different concentration was mixed with 50 μL of AAP (10 mM), and then the mixture was adjusted to pH 8.0 and was incubated in a water bath at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, 150 μL of freshly prepared DA (60 mM) and 20 μL of R-CDs (0.10 mg/mL) were added. The solution was diluted to be 3 mL with Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH 10.0) and further incubated at room temperature for 25 min. The fluorescence spectra with excitation wavelengths of 420 nm and 520 nm were recorded for the evaluation of ALP activity.

Human serum samples were provided by healthy adult volunteers in our laboratory. The experiment was carried out in the light of the relevant laws and institutional guidelines of China (WS/T225-2002). Informed consent was obtained from all subjects for experimentations with human subjects and the experiments were approved by the corresponding Institutional Committee of Nanning Normal University. Firstly, 500 μL of fresh human serum sample was combined with 1.5 mL of solution containing 4.0 mM of Na2EDTA and 2.0 M HClO4. After incubation for 30 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 g for 20 min to separate big proteins. Then, the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter, and the filtrate was stored at 4 °C. Before adding AA and ALP, the pretreated human serum sample was diluted for 100 times with Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH 8.0), and 1 mL of NEM (1 mM) was added directly into the diluted human serum samples. The AA standard solution was added into the pretreated human serum samples to obtain final concentrations of 5, 15, and 25 μM. The ALP standard solution was added into the pretreated human serum samples to get final activities of 0.5, 3, and 6 U/L. The concentrations of AA and ALP were detected by using our probe through the standard addition recovery method.

The size and the shape of R-CDs are characterized by TEM visibly and firstly. As shown in Fig. 1 A, R-CDs have good dispersibility in aqueous solution and possess a relatively uniform spherical shape. The average particle size of R-CDs is about 2.54 nm via the Gaussian fitting by calculating the sizes of about 100 particles (Fig. 1B). In addition, the particle size of R-CDs is dispersed mainly in the narrow range of 1.6–4.4 nm, indicating the uniform particle size distribution. The high-resolution TEM image illustrates that one R-CD particle has a crystal lattice of 0.21 nm (Inset in Fig. 1A), which is attributed to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [33]. This phenomenon indicates that the interior structure of R-CDs has a high degree of carbonization. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of R-CDs shows a characteristic peak at about 24.0° (Fig. 1C), which corresponds to the (002) plane of the crystalline graphitic carbon [33]. All these results suggest the mainly graphitic structure and the good crystalline conformation in our R-CDs.

FT-IR is further used to explore the functional groups on the surface of R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1D, the absorption peaks between 3550 and 3000 cm−1 are belonging to the characteristic absorption peaks of O–H and N–H of R-CDs [34]. The absorption peak at 1664.5 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and aromatic ring [35,36]. The absorption peak at 1512.8 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibration of aromatic ring [37]. The absorption peak at 1266.1 cm−1 stems from the stretching vibration of C–O [37]. The absorption peaks at 1174.8, 1097.5, and 1007.9 cm−1 imply the stretching vibration of B–O–H and the equivalent C–F bond, the bending vibration of C–B and the semi-ionic C–F bond, and the deformation vibration of B–O–H, respectively [7,38]. The absorption peak at 828.1 cm−1 suggests the bending vibration of aromatic N–H [37]. The absorption peak at 711.4 cm−1 expresses the bending vibration of C–N [39]. Such result suggests that the surface of R-CDs is rich in hydrophilic functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen elements.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is continuously used to analyze the valence and the composition of the incoming elements of R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the full XPS spectrum of R-CDs shows five different peaks of B 1s (192.62 eV), C 1s (284.84 eV), N 1s (399.47 eV), O 1s (531.81 eV), and F 1s (687.50 eV), respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 2B that the high-resolution C 1s spectrum shows four characteristic peaks, which are attributed to the C–B bond at 283.90 eV, the CC/C–C bonds at 284.58 eV, the C–O bond at 285.38 eV, and the CO/C–F bonds at 288.10 eV, respectively [40,41]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum shows two peaks (Fig. 2C), which are derived from the pyridinic N at 399.2 eV and the pyrrolic N at 400.0 eV, respectively [41]. Three characteristic peaks appeared at the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) stem from the CO bond at 531.43 eV, the C–O/NO bonds at 532.37 eV, and the C–OH/C–O–C bonds at 533.14 eV, respectively [42]. It can be seen from Fig. 2E that two peaks in the high-resolution B 1s spectrum represent the B–C bond at 191.91 eV and the B–O bond at 192.83 eV [43], respectively. Finally, the high-resolution F 1s spectrum shows two characteristic peaks (Fig. 2F), which are attributed to the semi-ionic C–F bond at 684.90 eV and the covalent C–F bond at 687.70 eV, respectively [44]. The XPS spectra of R-CDs prove the existences of C, N, O, B, and F elements. These R-CDs are rich in the hydrophilic amino and carboxyl functional groups, resulting in the excellent aqueous solubility of them for further biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical investigations of R-CDs and PDA
ce:section.ce:para: Our R-CDs are prepared via one-step hydrothermal method by using N-(4-amino phenyl) acetamide and (2,3-difluoro phenyl) boronic acid as precursors. These two aromatic precursors can reduce the energy gap by generating sp2 domain, which is beneficial to the preparation of CDs with long emission wavelength [45]. The doping of N and B elements and the existence of primary amine group can enhance the fluorescence intensity of such CDs [45]. Combining with the good aqueous solubility, the low cost, and the low toxicity, these two chemicals were used to prepare R-CDs. In order to obtain R-CDs with high optical quality, some important reaction conditions are needed to be optimized firstly. As exhibited in Fig. S1, the maximum fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of R-CDs is obtained after the hydrothermal treatment of 0.15 g of N-(4-amino phenyl) acetamide and 0.07 g of (2,3-difluoro phenyl) boronic acid at 180 °C for 6 h. The absolute fluorescence QY of R-CDs reaches to 12.9% by the integrating sphere via the equation of QY = ∫L emission/(∫E solvent – ∫E sample) [7]. Herein, L emission is the number of photons emitted by R-CDs, and E solvent and E sample mean the number of the excitation photons of the solvent and R-CDs, respectively.

The optical properties of R-CDs are evaluated by UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 A, R-CDs have a typical and sharp absorption peak at 249 nm, which may be derived from the π→π* transition of the CC bond [46]. The broad absorption band with peak position of 517 nm is derived from the n→π* transition of both CO and C–O/C–N bonds [11]. The light red R-CDs solution emits pink fluorescence under UV lamp irradiation with wavelength of 365 nm (Inset in Fig. 3A). As shown in Fig. 3B, there is a slight blue-shift (about 8 nm) in the emission spectrum of R-CDs under the excitation of increasing wavelength from 480 to 550 nm, which may ascribe to the enhanced steric hindrance effect in R-CDs [47]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of R-CDs are 520 and 602 nm separately, and the excitation wavelength is basically consistent with the characteristic absorption peak in the UV–vis spectrum of R-CDs. It is further seen from Fig. 3C that the average fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs is calculated as 1.06 ns according to the fitting equation of <τ> = (∑τ i 2 b i)/(∑τ i b i) via time-correlated single-photo counting system, where τ is the decay time of R-CDs and b is the pre-exponential factor [7]. The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs remained almost unchanged in solution even at high concentration of NaCl (3 M), wide pH range of 3–11, and long UV light irradiation of 120 min (Fig. S2). The above results confirm the excellent optical stability of R-CDs, which lays a solid foundation for the construction of R-CDs-based fluorescent sensing platform for both AA and ALP activity.

On the other hand, abundant PDA products with different mass-to-charge values are existed in the MALDI-TOF mass spectrum of PDA (Fig. S3), which suggests the generation of several PDA multimers. As further exhibited in Fig. 3D, the average particle size of these PDA multimers is around 200 nm, and these PDA multimers are almost spherical shape and are dispersed separately in aqueous solution. These phenomena confirm the construction of PDA by the self-polymerization and cyclization of DA in alkaline condition (pH 10.0) [48,49]. As shown in Fig. 3E, PDA possesses two strong absorption peaks at 242 and 275 nm, which may be derived from the π→π* transition of CC bond [46]. The small and broad absorption peak at 430 nm may be derived from the n→π* transition of CO bond [50]. PDA presents a light color under the irradiation of sunlight, while it emits a bright green fluorescence under UV lamp irradiation with wavelength of 365 nm (Inset in Fig. 3E). It can be seen from Fig. 3F that the fluorescence intensity of PDA is changed as the excitation wavelength increases from 380 to 450 nm. The maximum emission intensity of PDA is obtained, when the excitation and the emission wavelengths are 420 and 517 nm, respectively. The optimal excitation wavelength is basically consistent with the characteristic absorption peak in UV–vis spectrum of PDA. The emission lights of R-CDs and PDA are totally different, and the differential emission wavelength between them is as long as 85 nm. These advantages are beneficial to the construction of ratiometric fluorescent strategy based on R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility on AA detection and ALP activity sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The molecular interaction between R-CDs and PDA is vital for the construction of such ratiometric fluorescent probe, so we explored the interaction mechanism of the R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite through spectroscopic methods and Zeta potential measurement. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence decay curve of R-CDs keeps unchanged and the average fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs is still 1.06 ns with the addition of PDA, which eliminates the electron transfer and the energy transfer processes between R-CDs and PDA [51]. The Zeta potential values of both R-CDs and PDA are measured to be −12.5 and −10.4 mV, respectively. Therefore, due to the strong electrostatic repulsive force between both negatively charged R-CDs and negatively charged PDA, the distance of R-CDs with PDA is longer than 10 nm probably, also eliminating the energy transfer mechanism of the R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite. As exhibited in Fig. 4 A, the UV–vis absorption band of PDA overlaps with the excitation spectrum of R-CDs mainly and the emission spectrum of R-CDs to some extent, indicating that the fluorescence of R-CDs may be quenched by PDA through the IFE process [31,52]. To further verify the IFE process between R-CDs and PDA, we recorded the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs affected by PDA which produced by DA in alkaline condition (pH 10.0), and the fluorescence intensity was continuously corrected by using both the Parker and Barnes model and the Lakowicz model [53,54]. As shown in Fig. 4B and Tables S1 and S2, the deviations from the linearity converge are almost zero for PDA no matter the correction equations, reconfirming the mainly IFE process during the fluorescence quenching process of R-CDs by PDA.

R-CDs exhibit excellent optical properties and possess abundant hydrophilic surficial groups, so R-CDs have great potential application in the construction of numerous chemical and biological sensors. The feasibility of this ratiometric fluorescent probe for AA and ALP activity sensing was investigated, and the results were shown in Fig. 4C. It is obvious that R-CDs emit strong fluorescence with peak position of 602 nm under 520 nm light excitation but show weak fluorescence spectrum under 420 nm light excitation (black curves in Fig. 4C). On the contrary, DA is self-polymerized and cyclized in alkaline condition to form PDA with green fluorescence at 517 nm under 420 nm light excitation, and PDA quenches the fluorescence of R-CDs at 602 nm under 520 nm light excitation via the IFE process (red curves in Fig. 4C). The self-polymerization and cyclization of DA and the production of PDA are all inhibited by AA, so the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs is restored dramatically while the fluorescence intensity of PDA is decreased greatly (blue curves in Fig. 4C). Due to the efficient inhibition ability of AA on the production of PDA, such ratiometric fluorescent probe can be used to detect AA with high sensitivity. On the other hand, AA can be produced by the hydrolyzation of AAP under the catalysis of ALP, and the concentration of AA is correlated closely with the activity of ALP. The purple curves in Fig. 4C suggest that the AA, which is produced by the catalytic reaction between AAP and ALP, exhibits the equal influences on the fluorescence intensities of both R-CDs and PDA. Consequently, this assay can be expanded to selectively evaluate the activity of ALP through the specific enzyme-substrate interaction.

The activity of ALP is very important for the construction of such R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite system, so the enzymatic reaction rate of ALP was explored through the Michaelis-Menten equation by choosing PNPP as the substrate [55]. ALP can catalyze the hydrolyzation of PNPP to produce PNP, and the kinetic parameters of ALP are obtained from the enzymatic reaction kinetics of PNP produced by PNPP. Fig. 4D shows the relationship between the generation rate (V) of PNP at the corresponding time and the concentration of PNPP. It is clear that the changing trend of the curve basically follows the Michaelis-Menten equation pattern. According to the double reciprocal plot in Fig. 4E, the V value and the Michaelis constant (K m) value can be calculated as the formula of 1/V = K m/(V max [PNPP]) + 1/V max. It is obvious that V max = (368.04 ± 3.20) μM/h and K m = (271.68 ± 8.13) μM. These values are highly agreed with the reported value [56], which confirms that ALP has high kinetic characteristics and strong catalytic activity on the hydrolyzation of its substrates.

In order to achieve the best performance of our R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite system for AA detection, some detection parameters were systematically optimized. The results show that the optimal conditions for construction of R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite are as follows: (1) the amount of R-CDs of 0.10 mg/mL, (2) Tris-HCl buffer with pH 10.0, (3) the concentration of DA of 60 mM, (4) the volume of DA of 150 μL, and (5) the reaction time of 25 min (Fig. S4). After the addition of AA, the ratiometric fluorescence signal F602/F517 value keeps constant when the reaction time was 25 min (Fig. S5), thus the optimal detection time for AA is 25 min. Under the above detection conditions, the fluorescence spectrum of our R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite affected by AA was measured. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs at 602 nm is increased gradually while that of PDA at 517 nm is decreased gradually after the addition of AA with increasing concentration, which may be attributed to the efficient inhibition ability of AA on the generation of PDA [48,49]. Consequently, the ratiometric fluorescence signal F602/F517 value is increased gradually with the increasing concentration of AA (Fig. 5B). Especially, the F602/F517 value shows a good linear relationship with the concentration of AA increasing from 0.5 to 30 μM (Fig. 5C). The linear equation is expressed as F602/F517 = 0.0497 × [AA](μM) + 0.870 (R2 = 0.993), and the detection limit of AA is calculated to be 0.28 μM by the 3σ/S formula (σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal of R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite for eleven measurements and S is the slope of the linear plot, respectively). Compared with the related strategies for AA detection (Table S3), our R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite-based ratiometric fluorescence probe has an acceptable linear range and a lower detection limit. In addition, other amino acids and small biomolecules (including GSH) with 1 mM do not affect the F602/F517 value obviously, but 0.1 mM of AA can enhance the F602/F517 value to be more than three times comparably (Fig. 5D). Such phenomena reconfirm that only AA can inhibit the generation of PDA efficiently while GSH and other biomolecules do not possess such abilities [48,49], which may be ascribed to the much stronger reducing property of AA than GSH and some other scavengers for electrophilic and oxidant species. Furthermore, AA can form free radical cations through the electron transfer process and competitively prevent the spontaneous oxidative polymerization of dopamine [48,49]. All these results imply the excellent selectivity of our method for AA analysis. Especially, the F602/F517 value remains almost unchanged when these biomolecules and common inorganic salts are mixed with AA together (Table S4), implying that such ratiometric fluorescence probe can detect AA specifically in sophisticated biological matrix.

In order to achieve the best evaluation of ALP activity based on our R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite, the hydrolyzation of AAP by ALP is taken place under the following experimental conditions: (1) Tris-HCl buffer with pH 8.0, (2) reaction temperature of 37 °C, and (3) reaction time of 30 min (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. 6 A, as the activity of ALP increases from 0 to 15 U/L, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs at 602 nm is recovered gradually and the fluorescence intensity of PDA at 517 nm is decreased conversely. Such variation trend is almost the same with the changing trend caused by AA alone, which not only proves the enzymatic activity of ALP on the hydrolyzation of AAP but also provides the potential application of our method for evaluating ALP activity. It is subsequently seen from Fig. 6B that the ratiometric fluorescence signal F602/F517 value is increased with the increment of ALP activity from 0.05 to 15 U/L, and this F602/F517 value shows a good linear relationship with ALP activity in the range of 0.05–8 U/L (Fig. 6C). The linear regression equation is F602/F517 = 0.195 × [ALP](U/L) + 0.789 (R2 = 0.995), and the detection limit of ALP activity is calculated to be 0.044 U/L via the value of 3σ/S (σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal of R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite for eleven measurements and S is the slope of the linear plot, respectively). Compared with the related strategies for the evaluation of ALP activity (Table S5), this ratiometric fluorescence probe has a much wider linear range and a relatively lower detection limit. In addition, 100 U/L of common enzymes (AChE, trypsin, pepsin, GOx, uricase, papain, peroxidase, ChOx, lysozyme, and bromelain) affect the F602/F517 value very tiny, while 10 U/L of ALP can enhance the F602/F517 value to be more than three times (Fig. 6D). Such excellent selectivity of our method for ALP activity is mainly ascribed to the specific catalytic action between ALP and its substrate. These substances (enzymes, amino acids, and inorganic salts) do not change the F602/F517 value significantly when they are coexisted with 10 U/L of ALP in solution (Table S6), further suggesting the highly selective evaluation of ALP activity in complicated authentic samples.

To further evaluate the applicability of our R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for the analysis of AA and ALP activity in authentic samples, the determinations of AA concentration and ALP activity in diluted human serum samples were carried out. Our preliminary experimental result suggests that the ratiometric fluorescence signal F602/F517 value remains almost unchanged in such diluted human serum samples for different time, no matter NEM is present or absent (Fig. S7). In order to maintain the high accuracy of our method, 1 mL of NEM (1 mM) was still added into the diluted human serum samples directly. Due to the high selectivity of our method and the addition of thiol-blocking reagent NEM [19], some coexistences, such as GSH, GSSG, L-Cys, and homocysteine, do not affect the detections of AA and ALP in human serum samples.

Based on the linear equations of both AA and ALP analysis, the intrinsic amounts of AA and ALP in diluted human serum samples are discovered in the ranges of 0.54–2.36 μM and 0.698–0.948 U/L, respectively. Therefore, the intrinsic amounts of AA and ALP in original human serum samples are calculated to be in the ranges of 54–236 μM and 69.8–94.8 U/L, respectively. The concentration of AA and the activity of ALP in human serum samples are all in the reported normal ranges in human serum samples [14,19–21]. After adding different concentration of AA in these diluted human serum samples, the recovery of AA concentration is in the range of 96.0%–104.2% and the RSD value is in the range of 1.7%–4.2% (Table 1 ). Meanwhile, after adding different activity of ALP in such diluted human serum samples, the recovery of ALP activity is in the range of 95.4%–104.6% and the RSD value is in the range of 0.9%–3.5% (Table 1). The superior recovery values show that this ratiometric fluorescence probe can quantitatively analysis the concentration of AA and evaluate the ALP activity in biological samples with satisfactory results.

In summary, we synthesized R-CDs with absolute fluorescence QY of 12.9% and excellent optical property by one-step hydrothermal method without complicated separation and treatment processes, which provides a facile and economic method for the convenient and efficient preparation of R-CDs. Due to the IFE process between R-CDs and PDA, such R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite-based ratiometric fluorescent probe was established for the simultaneous detection of AA and ALP activity. The linear ranges of AA and ALP were 0.5–30 μM and 0.05–8 U/L separately, and the detection limits of AA and ALP were as low as 0.28 μM and 0.044 U/L, respectively. The designed platform has high detection accuracy and great recovery for the detection of spiked AA and ALP in human serum samples, which verifies the broad practical application of such R-CDs-based sensing platform in biological analysis. This is the initial ratiometric fluorescent probe based on R-CDs/PDA nanocomposite for AA and ALP detection by using the fluorescence of R-CDs as the output signal, and it also exploits a new direction for constructing such ratiometric fluorescence platform based on R-CDs with superior optical property.

Qi Xiao: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing - Review and Editing; Pingping Mu: Investigation, Validation, Writing - Review and Editing; Gan Ning: Investigation, Validation,; Wenqian Zhang: Investigation, Writing - Draft; Bo Li: Methodology, Investigation; Shan Huang: Resources, Supervision, Writing - Review and Editing.
